text: It was justified to attempt resection to prevent death from pericardial space invasion and catastrophic bleeding.
Access to the chest was triportal.
The first task was to assess pericardial invasion.
Therefore, a small hole was made in the pericardium away from the tumor, and the bipolar encele was then used to cut around the tumor, leaving a reasonable safety margin.
The use of a bipolar device safeguards against arrhythmia and is very safe to use on the pericardium.
It was evident that the tumor transgressed the pericardium but not involved the heart yet.
Where the tumor was near to the phrenic nerve, we opted to cut it with the scissors to avoid diaphragmatic paralysis.
However, Enseal is very safe near nerves from previous experience.
As can be seen clearly, the defect was big and can easily allow herniation of the heart, a potentially fatal complication.
Disconnection of the tumour from the pericardium allowed proceeding with left lower lobectomy in the usual way.
The aim of this video is to tell you how we dealt with the pericardial defect.
We decided to use a bovine pericardial patch.
A 10 by 10 centimeter graft was fashioned outside the chest and pushed in to eyeball the coverage.
The Sils Stitch device was used to suture the patch into position.
This is a clever device made by Medtronic Covidien, which has a small needle that shuttles from one end to the other, moving an Ethibon Zero suture from one side to the other.

We found this new indication for the instrument as this was not reported before In fact, we found the device very safe to take stitches around the heart and the reason for this is that the shuttle action of the needle is short distance calculated and protected by the other receiving end of the instrument so it is not actually bare to cause accidental damage to the exposed myocardium
Obviously, one has to keep an eye on the sharp end of the needle all the time, but the chance of accidental injury is very small.
Compared to stitching the patch using a small needle and a conventional needle holder, it is hard for me to imagine the safety of such a strategy.
Nevertheless, in the hands of expert giants such as Mr. Bill Walker, this was achieved before.
However, I would not recommend it for the average VAT surgeon.

even robotically this would be difficult and the difficulty does not arise from needle control but from the beating heart with unpredictable jerking into your needle several interrupted stitches suitable interspace to anchor the patch are laid down again care must be exercised not to include the phrenic nerve in one of these stitches

From time to time, the operator needs to eyeball the general position of the patch so as to be able to judge placement of the stitches The SILS stitch made this task very easy and very safe In fact, the technique could be extended to suture a patch for any other reason in the chest For example, a diaphragmatic hernia, especially when end-to-end closure, is not possible without tension
The operative strategy was to assess whether the tumour could be freed from the pericardium, and if so, to proceed with lower lobectomy.
If this was not possible, then bailing out from a VATS procedure would be less traumatic than from a full thoracotomy.
The patient understood this is not going to be a curative resection, but at least will prevent invasion into the heart.
A pericardial effusion with its complications would have ensued, no doubt.
In conclusion, the use of the Silsu Stitch device has made it easy and safe to stitch down a bovine pericardial patch to cover a large pericardial defect.
